paragraphs.context: semr SEMI E170-0520 SPECIFICATION FOR SECURED FOUNDATION OF RECIPE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM (SFORMS) This Standard was technically approved by the Jnfonnation & Control Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Rc,,icws Subcommittee on December 12, 2019 and January 30, 2020. Available at www.scmivicws.org and www.scmi.org in May 2020; originally published July 2014; previously published April 2019. 1 Purpose 1.1 The purpose of this Standard is to provide a standardized 'secure and efficient recipe operation mechanism between Recipe Management System (RMS) and equipment' which is used as a foundation of RMS for semiconductor manufacturing systems or similar ones. 2 Scope 2.1 The scope of th is Standard is to define a secure and efficient recipe operation mechanism intended to be used as a foundation of RMS. 2.2 The scope of this Standard is to define an extended recipe identification scheme and its usage that identifies recipes from multiple aspects including uniqueness and security. NOTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility of the users of the Documents to establish appropriate safety and health practices. and determine the applicability of regulatory or other limitations prior to use. 3 Limitations 3.1 None. 4 Referenced Standards and Documents 4.1 SEMI Srandards and Safety Guidelines SEMI E30 - Specification for the Generic Model for Communications and Control of Manufacturing Equipment (GEM) SEMI E39 - Specification for Object Services Standard: Concepts, Behavior, and Services SEMI E40

[SEMI E30] 6 Conventions 6.1 Objects 6.1.1 Whenever the equipment is required to know about specific kinds of entities, and required to manage information concerning these entities, it is useful to treat these entities as objects that comply with the basic requirements of SEMI E39 Object Services Standard (OSS). This is especially true whenever there are a large number of objects of a given type or when the entities are transient rather than permanent. lo both cases. it is difficult to describe a general way for the host and equipment to specify which particular entity is referenced and to get information related only to a specific one out of many. 6.1.2 By defining these entities as objects that comply with OSS, it is only necessary for the host to specify the type of object and its specific identifier in order to inquire about one or more properties of the specific entity of interest. 6.1.3 Objecr Properties 6.1.3.1 A property (attribute) is information about an individual object that is presented as a name/value pair. The name is a formally reserved text string that represents the property. and the value is the current setting for that property. 6.1.3.2 Properties shall be accessible to the host via the service GetAttr. Equipment must maintain state models for each of the required state models as defined in this Standard. Equipment shall maintain individual and unique state models for each logical entity instantiated or physical entity in the equipment that has state models associated with it. 6.2.6.2 Representation as the Host View - A state model represents the host's view of the equipment and does not necessarily describe the internal equipment operation. All state model transitions shall be mapped sequentially into the appropriate internal equipment collection events that satisfy the requirements of those transitions. Lo certain implementations, the equipment may enter a state and have already satisfied all of the conditions required by the state models in this Standard for transition to another state. In this case, the equipment makes the required transition without any additional actions in this situation. 6.2.6.3 Additional Substates - Some equipment may need to include additional substates other than those in this Standard. Additional substates may be added but shall not change the defined state transitions in this Standard. All expected transitions between states in

For example, if the equipment has two load ports and the load port state model defines IO transitions. there must be exactly IO event identifiers for each load port transfer state model but not IO for each physical load port. The information identifying the physical entity or logical entity undergoing the transition will be contained within the associated event report. 6.2.6.5 Events - All state transitions in this Standard. unless otherwise specified, shall correspond to collection events. More explicitly, there must be a unique collection event for each state transition. 6.2.6.6 Events for Multiple AND Substates - ln case a state model is defined with multiple AND substates, the equipment may report all state entry events with only one collection event. 6.2.6.7 Events for Conditional Path - In case conditional paths are defined in the state model, it is not necessary to report any state transition(s) until a terminal state is reached at which time each transition used to reach that

Once instantiated, the object is considered destroyed (no longer instantiated) if the response to such queries is 'unknown object'. 6.3.2 Object Identifier (Obj/D) - The purpose of an Object Identifier is to allow references to an object within the system. The object identifier is assigned when an object is instantiated and should be unchanged or persistent until the end of the object lifecycle. The Object Identifier shall be unique at the equipment during lifecycle of the object. 6.4 Transactions and Services 6.4.1 Definition of Service- Services are functions or methods that may be provided by either the equipment or the host. A service message may be either a request message, which always requires a response, or a notification message that does not require a response. 6.4.1.1 Notification Message Service otification type messages are initiated by the service provider (e.g., the equipment) and the provider does not expect to get a response from the service user. 6.4.1.2 Request Message Service - Request messages

paragraphs.context: operation. RMS downloads the required recipes at the optimum timing prior to the associated PJ creation request and may retain the recipes in the cache for the next use or may delete them from the cache. considering the balance of security and recipe transaction efficiency. Cache Mode (Optional)-Equipment-controlled cache operation. Equipment keeps allowed numbers of recently used recipe for allowed duration in PRC. Equipment looks up PRC for the required recipes for PJ creation and queries the recipes to RMS if they are not in PRC. Full Download Mode (Optional) - RMS downloads required recipes every time just before MES demands a PJ creation and deletes the recipes immediately after the use. TI1is mode gives recipe check overhead to equipment every time. Full Query Mode (Optional)-Equipment queries required recipes every time upon PJ creation and deletes them after use. 7.4.4.2 Pre-Execution Check Operarion (Optional) - Pre-execution check is not logically required for PRC which is properly implemented and operated, however, may be required to help deployment path from conventional Pre execution-check based system or for double check purpose in some cases. answers.text: Equipment-controlled cache operation

qas.question: How does Full Download Mode operate regarding recipe downloads and deletion?

answers.text: RMS downloads required recipes every time just before MES demands a PJ creation and deletes the recipes immediately after the use

qas.question: What action occurs when Full Query Mode is utilized?

answers.text: Equipment queries required recipes every time upon PJ creation and deletes them after use

qas.question: Under what circumstances might Pre-Execution Check be necessary?

answers.text: to help deployment path from conventional Pre execution-check based system or for double check purpose in some cases

qas.question: What is the process involved in a Pre-Exe Check?

answers.text: Equipment uploads check information of the required recipes to RMS for consistency check upon PJ creation, and RMS responds with an affirmative message or with a negative message which contains correct recipes

qas.question: What does RMS do with required recipes before a PJ creation request?

answers.text: downloads the required recipes at the optimum timing prior to the associated PJ creation request and may retain the recipes in the cache for the next use or may delete them from the cache

RMS uses ClearOperation transaction to execute Cache Clear Operation. The equipment executes Cache Clear Operatio' triggered by the cases listed below. This operation is used in case the recipes in PRC are deemed inconsistent with the recipes in RMS. RMS or the privileged user shall trigger Cache Clear Operation in case there is a known mismatch between recipes in PRC and the recipes on the RMS. RMS or the privileged user may trigger Cache Clear Operation in case they deem that mismatch is probable between recipes in PRC and the recipes on the RMS (such as after maintenance). RMS or the privileged user may trigger Cache Clear Operation in case they deem there is a security concern on leaving the recipes in PRC. A user may trigger Cache Clear Operation

These have been supplied by the global technical committee to enhance the usage of the Standard or Safety Guideline. 1 Purpose 1.1 The purpose of this Standard is to provide a communication scheme for exchanges of carrier logistics related information, especially predictive infom1ation. between equipment and the factory system in order to support seamless cascading of carriers for continuous processing of equipment in semiconductor fabrication systems or similar ones. 1.2 The purpose of this Standard is to provide models which represent carrier logistics management status in equipment to exchange predictive carrier logistics information in order to facilitate better synchronization with the factory system. 2 Scope

paragraphs.context: of carriers for continuous processing of equipment in semiconductor fabrication systems or similar ones. 1.2 The purpose of this Standard is to provide models which represent carrier logistics management status in equipment to exchange predictive carrier logistics information in order to facilitate better synchronization with the factory system. 2 Scope 2.1 This Standard defines a model which represents logistics management of a carrier in equipment as Carrier Logistics Job (CLJ). CU includes the following functionalities. State management of logistics management of a carrier Prediction management including submission, resubmission and withdrawal upon a change of predicted information such as time or load port assignment Carrier ID assignment Load port assignment 2.2 This Standard defines a model which represents management of multiple CUs in equipment as Carrier Flow Job (CFJ). CFJ includes the following functionalities. Order management of CUs per process order Prediction management ofCUs per carrier logistics order Carrier scheduling Load port scheduling including a queuing of multiple CUs on one load port Load port arbitration to CLJs for Internal Buffer Equipment 2.3 This Standard covers specifications for services to and events from CU and CFJ models. 2.4 This Standard covers PCL specifications for both Fixed Buffer Equipment and Internal Buffer Equipment. 2.5 This Standard covers compatibility with non-slot-integrity operation. NOTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. It is the responsibility of the users of the Documents to establish appropriate safety and health practices, and determine the applicability of regulatory

SEMI E171.0524 SEMI 2014, 2024 semr 3 Limitations 3.1 On the Fly Control Purpose-This Standard is not for metrics (especially not for postmortem analysis) of waste times that may be contained in several time slots defined in this Standard due to the design of equipment and/or fab system. It only cares about synchronization bet\veen equipment and fab system. Those metrics should be standardized separately if needed. 3.2 Best Effort Basis - The requirements regarding prediction are on a best effort basis. as a prediction has limited accuracy in nature; moreover. in some cases prediction itself might not be provided. 3.3 No Prediction Algorithm Covered - This Standard does not cover calculation algorithms for predictions. 3.4 Only for Cn"tical Equipmellt - This Standard is intended to be used for equipment for which carrier logistics are critical. and, application of this Standard especially prediction portions may not be needed for equipment to which carrier logistics are not critical. 3.5 Not/or Physical Synchroni=ation of load Pon - This Standard is not intended to provide physical status of load ports. 4 Referenced Standards and Documents 4.1 SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines SEMI E 15 - Specification for Tool

Load Queued: This state is introduced mainly for lnternal Buffer Equipment to represent the situation in which Load Request Prediction or Load Stagnation Prediction is done, however the equipment is not capable to accept a carrier for the prediction yet (typically because no space is available in Internal Buffer yet). ln case CLJs are assigned to load ports in order by their predicted times and there is no possibility of dead lock. Load Request state may be used instead of Load Queued state for simplicity. Refer to Appendix l, Internal Buffer Equipment for an example. Load Request: This state is introduced to represent the situation in which the equipment is requesting load of a carrier. Normally, during CEW, other load ports hold required carriers for cascading of wafers. A carrier has overhead times such as confirmation of carrier ID, opening of the door, confirmation of wafer existence on slots, and creation of process job before the wafers become ready for process, overhead times such as closing of the door, purging with inert gas, and undock after the process completion, and equipment has a wafer queue. Due to those overhead times, Carrier Valid requires Handover Periods. The Handover Period is determined by equipment according to its configuration and operation. The host is strongly recommended to compare the host clock and equipment clock, and to understand the reported time per equipment clock in host time per host clock. 8.3 Prediction Resubmission Threshold for PCL - PCL allows resubmission of predictions to report updated value. When the carrier is not unloaded by this predicted timing, subsequent carrier load (or unload in internal buffer equipment) may be delayed and likely to cause the stagnation. SEMI E171-0524 SEMI 2014, 2024 semr I 0.3.3 Carrier ID Assignment (Optional) - An optional function which manages the assignment of a carrier to the CLJ. In case neither check nor negotiation of the order of carrier handling is done through PCL functionalities, this option may not be required. I 0.3.3.1 late Carrier Assignment - A function to assign carrier after CLJ is created. This enables the use of Load Request function, in which equipment basically does not know which carrier will be assigned. I 0.3.3.2 Carrier Type (Optional) - An optjonal attribute to indicate which kind of carrier is dealt with the CLJ. I 0.3.4 load Port Assignment (Optional) - An optional function which manages the assignment of a load port to the CLJ. A CLJ uses load port in order to perform physical handoff operation between AMHS at the last part of Load Request or Unload Request state. I 0.3.4.1 Dynamic Load Port Assignment (Oprional) - An optional function which creates CLJ without load port assignment

I 0.3.4.1 Dynamic Load Port Assignment (Oprional) - An optional function which creates CLJ without load port assignment and assigns a load port when a coordination is done. This function is mainly for Internal Buffer Equipment. Since load ports in Internal Buffer Equipment are places which can temporarily be used for both Load and Unload, assignment of them should be done just before Load/Unload operation for more effective scheduling. ScOI Load Queued Optional The CU is queued in CF J. but is not requesting load of a carrier yet. This state may not be required when Load Request Prediction is not used. Sc02 Load Request The CU is requesting load of a carrier. This state may not be required when Load Stagnation Prediction is not used. This state is not equal to ready to load. Logical readiness to load is determined by the position of the CU in CFJ. Physical readiness to load shall be confirmed by using SEMI E87 functionalities. Fixed Buffer Equipment: ln normal cases, the CU is assigned on a load port. Internal Buffer Equipment: The CU may or may not be assigned on a load port at the beginning of this slate. Multiple CUs may be in Load Request state considering a dynamic use of multiple load ports for multiple CLJs in AMHS arrival order. The arbitration algorithm is not defined

SEMI E171-0524 SEMI 2014, 2024 semr No. Mnemonic State Defin111011 Comments Sc03 Carrier Valid The CU is loaded with a carrier. The carrier is waiting for the use or in use, and is valid for the equipment. This state starts with the end of SEMI E84 sequence for load, and ends when the use of the carrier at FIMS port (Load port for Fixed Buffer Equipment, internal FIMS port for lntcmal Buffer Equipment) is completed.. the door is closed, undockcd or ready to undock, and the equipment has no operation other than unload. Sc04 Unload Request The CU is requesting unload of the carrier assigned to the CU. This state is not equal to ready to unload. Logical readiness to unload is determined by the priority of the CU in CF J. Physical readiness to unload shall be confirmed by using SEMI E87 functionalities. Fixed Buffer Equipment: ln normal cases. the CU stays assigned on the same load port. Internal Buffer Equipment: The CU may not be assigned on a load port at the beginning of this state when arbitration of load ports to CUs is done by the arrival order information from AMHS. paragraphs.context: Overview .......................................................................................................................................... 5 8 Prerequisites ............................................................................................................................................................... 8 9 Requirements .............................................................................................................................................................. 8 10 Test Methods .......................................................................................................................................................... 15 I I Related Documents ................................................................................................................................................. 15 Appendix 1 .................................................................................................................................................................. 16 SEMI E172.0225 SEMI 2015, 2025 semr 1 Purpose 1.1 The purpose of this Specification is to define the fonnat and content of a dataset that describes key elements of the SECS-II interface for any production equipment. This is a type of documentation, called the SECS Equipment Data Dictionary (SEDD). It is primarily intended as a means for an equipment supplier to provide such documentation to its customers. The customers are expected to use this documentation to streamline the development of the factory software that communicates with the production equipment. 1.2 This Specification is not intended to eliminate the need for traditional paper (or equivalent) equipment interface documentation. This is a supplement to that documentation. 2 Scope 2.1 The SEDD is intended to be a data set that docwnents key elements of a SECS-II interface. The form in which this data is provided is intended to be comprehensible to both humans and software applications. NOTICE: SEMI Standards and Safety Guidelines do not purport to address all safety issues associated with their use. This contains some descriptive information about the SEDD and the production equipment it represents. Its purpose is to allow the user of the SEDD understand to which production equipment the SEDD applies. 7.5.5 The next six parts are the core of the SEDD. They provide the detailed definitions of the events, variables, and alanns that the equipment can communicate. 7.5.6 The final part is the VariableFonnats element. It is a list of formats that apply to the various variables. 1.6 Variab/eFormats 7.6.1 Each variable definition in the SEDD (i.e., StatusVariable, Data Variable, EquipmentConstant, or RVP) includes a 'Format' field. Where the other types of variables have fixed names for a given production equipment, RVPs can have names that vary according to usage. 7.7.2 For example, consider a multichamber equipment with the following characteristics: The process chambers can be utilized in varying sequences A chamber can be used more than once in the sequence Chamber A has a temperature setting that is available as an RVP 7. 7.2.1 If a chamber can be used more than once and it can appear in any step during the processing sequence. then RVP naming can be a problem. If Chamber A is used in Step I and Step 4 of the process sequence and a different temperature is specified for each instance, then a single RVP named A Temp' would not be sufficient. SEMI E172.0225 SEMI 2015, 2025 semr 7.7.2.2 However, if the step number were added, then the two values could be specified as SteplATemp and Step4A Temp. 7.7.3 The SEDD schema allows for such flexible naming of RVPs. An RVP name can be defined as either a fixed name or a pattern. This is designated using the Boolean RVPNamePattem attribute of the RVP element. It is recommended that new equipment implementations define their SECS-II variable, collection event, and alarm names to use the same value as the well-known names. 9.5.1 RecipeType 9.5.7.1 The RecipeVariableParameters section of the SEDD schema contains the RVP definitions. It also contains an attribute named RecipeType. 9.5.7.2 Production equipment may support more than one type of recipe component. Each type of recipe component supported may support a different set of R VPs. The Recipe Type attribute allows the equipment supplier to differentiate among the different types of recipe components it might support. All RecipeTypes are assigned by the equipment supplier. 9.5.7.2.1 For example, a production equipment might support sequence' recipe components that reference subordinate recipes for each type of module present (e.g., 'Etch', 'Clean'. etc.). It might also support 'Maintenance' or 'Calibration' recipe components. Each of these (i.e., Sequence, Etch, Clean, Maintenance, Calibration) could be declared as Recipe Types. 9.5.7.3 There can be multiple RecipeVariablePammeters elements in the SEDD. Each represents a different Recipe Type. Each has its own set of RVP definitions. Please refer to the design in the SEDD schema.

SEMI E1 72-0225 SEMI 2015, 2025 1 2 semr 9.5.8.2 RVP ames are sometimes affected by the design of the equipment recipe. 9.5.8.2.1 For example. consider a production equipment that contains multiple modules-Clean and Process. A normal sequence might be I) Clean, 2) Process, 3) Clean. The Clean module might talce a 'Clean Time' parameter. The problem would be to differentiate between the parameter value intended for the Clean of Step I and that of Step 3. One solution would be to generate RVP names that included the step number (i.e., Step I Clean Time and Step3CleanTime). 9.5.8.2.2 For a different equipment recipe, the sequence might change and Clean would occur in Step 2. Thus, the RVP name is dependent on the specific equipment recipe. The RVP name could be written generaJly as a pattern, such as Step&CleanTime where placeholder & = 1-9. Thus. if applied to first step, the actual RVP name would become Step I Clean Time. 9.5.8.2.3 The SEDD schema provides for this type of situation by

paragraphs.context: semr SEMI E173-0721 SPECIFICATION FOR XML SECS-II MESSAGE NOTATION (SMN) This Standard was technically approved by the Information & Control Global Technical Committee. This edition was approved for publication by the global Audits and Reviews Subcommittee on April 30, 2021. Available at www.scmiviews.org and www.semi.org in July 2021; originally published April 2015; previously published October 2018. Table of Contents I Purpose ....................................................................................................................................................................... 2 2 Scope .......................................................................................................................................................................... 2 3 Limitations .................................................................................................................................................................. 2 4 Referenced Standards and Documents ....................................................................................................................... 2 5 Tenninology ............................................................................................................................................................... 3 6 Conventions ................................................................................................................................................................ 3 7 Requirements .............................................................................................................................................................. 4 8 Test Methods ............................................................................................................................................................ 12 Appendix I .................................................................................................................................................................. 13 Related lnfonnation I .................................................................................................................................................. 15 SEMI E173.0721 SEMI 2015, 2021 semr 1 Purpose 1.1 The content of SECS-II messages are defined in SEMI E5. SEMI Equipment Communication Standards 2 Message Content (SECS-II). SECS-II messages can be transmitted to and from and equipment using either the protocol defined in SEMI E4, Equipment Communication Standards I Messages Transfer (SECS-I) or the protocol defined in SEMI E37, High Speed Message Transfer (HSMS) Generic Services. 1.2 The purpose of this Specification is to define a standardized notation to represent the content of SECS-II messages or the total message when SECS-II messages are sent or received using the SECS-I or HSMS protocol. SECS-II Message Notation (SMN) is intended for use when documenting and logging SECS-II messages. The notation is intended to be used when documenting the purpose. structure. and sequence of SECS-II messages. The notation is also intended to be used when logging SECS-II message communication

paragraphs.context: using the SECS-I or HSMS protocol. SECS-II Message Notation (SMN) is intended for use when documenting and logging SECS-II messages. The notation is intended to be used when documenting the purpose. structure. and sequence of SECS-II messages. The notation is also intended to be used when logging SECS-II message communication traffic. 2 Scope 2.1 SMN as described in this Specification standardizes an XML notation that can be used for both the logging and documentation of SECS-II messages. The rules of this notation are primarily specified using an XML schema. Any logging and documentation that comply with SM are created in an XML format that passes SMN schema validation. 2.2 When SECS-II message transactions are logged using SMN, the XML represents the content, structure, order, and additional transaction details of sent and received messages. Logging might include only the SECS-II message content. Logging might also include the protocol message header. 2.3 When documenting SECS-II messages, the XML represents the content, structure, and description of the SECS II messages. Message scenarios can also be documented to indicate the order and content of messages in a sequence. Typically. SECS-II documentation includes only the SECS-II message content. Some of the elements and attributes in the schema file are exclusively for documenting SECS-II messages. SEMI E173.0721 SEMI 2015, 2021 semr 7.3.2.2 Either HSMS or SECS-I protocol level messages can be embedded anywhere within a sequence by using the HSMSMessage and SECS-I Message elements. HSMSMessage and SECS-I Message elements can be used to log the entire message header and message data in its original format as sent or received.
